Community  ·  Poverty  ·  Practical Giving

Beyond the Dollar What Actually Helps a Panhandler

You hand over a buck, they nod, you walk on. Both of you know it isn't enough. So what is?

There's a woman I used to see on the same corner for years. Same spot, same sign, same practiced look of exhaustion. She didn't look destitute exactly — she looked established. Like she had figured something out that the rest of us hadn't. I don't know her story. Neither did anyone else who dropped coins into her cup. And that, it turns out, is the starting problem.

We give money to panhandlers the way we swat at mosquitoes — reflexively, imprecisely, and without any expectation that it will actually solve anything. Most of us suspect as much. We just don't know what else to do.

Here's what the research says — and what it suggests you actually can do.

94% spend panhandling money on food as their #1 expense
$300 median monthly income from panhandling alone
70% would prefer a minimum-wage job over panhandling

First, Forget What You Think You Know

The popular mythology around panhandlers is almost entirely wrong, and it matters, because wrong assumptions lead to useless or even harmful responses.

Myth
They're all spending it on booze and drugs.

A 2013 survey of 146 panhandlers in San Francisco found that 94% spent their money on food. Yes, 44% also used some portion on drugs or alcohol occasionally — but the primary expense, overwhelmingly, was eating. The largest peer-reviewed systematic review confirmed the same pattern: food first, then tobacco, then substance use, in that order.

Myth
They're making great money.

They aren't. A rigorous review across 38 studies found the typical range is $20–$60 per day. The median monthly take from panhandling alone works out to around $300 — roughly $3,600 a year. You can't rent a room on $3,600 a year in any American city.

Myth
They choose this life.

About 70% of panhandlers say they would prefer a minimum-wage job. The words they use are telling: "steady income," "get off the street," "feel normal." The barrier isn't desire. It's a cascade of missing prerequisites that makes the path back to conventional employment nearly impassable without outside help.

What panhandlers spend their money on
Percentage reporting each as a regular expense — San Francisco study, 2013 (n=146)
Food
94%
Tobacco
~60%
Drugs or alcohol
44%
Clothing / hygiene
~32%
Shelter / lodging
~25%

What Actually Works: The Cascade Problem

The reason a dollar doesn't help much isn't that it's a dollar — it's that the underlying problem isn't a dollar-sized problem. Panhandlers who want to exit street life face what social workers call a cascade of prerequisites, each one blocked by the previous.

The Prerequisite Chain

✗No job without an address to put on an application
✗No address without money for a deposit
✗No deposit without a bank account to save it in
✗No bank account without a permanent address to verify
✗No address without an ID to register one
✗No ID without a birth certificate to prove identity
✗No birth certificate without knowing how to request one or money for the fee

Each step depends on the one before it. Giving someone cash puts them further along in none of these steps. What does move the needle is knowing where to point someone when they're ready to move.

What You Can Actually Do

1

Know Your Local Rescue Mission or Day Center

This is the single most useful thing most people can do. Every major city has at least one organization — often called a rescue mission, day center, or drop-in center — that provides far more than food. The good ones offer:

What these organizations actually provide

•Help obtaining birth certificates, Social Security cards, and state ID
•Case managers who navigate shelter and housing applications
•Job placement assistance and vocational training
•Substance use treatment referrals
•A physical address for mail and banking purposes

Write the name and address on a notecard or keep it in your phone. When you give someone a dollar, you can also say: "The [Name] Mission is on [Street]. They can help you get your ID." That takes ten seconds and costs nothing. On the right day, it's the most valuable thing anyone has said to that person in months.

He didn't need more dollar bills. He needed someone to help him get a piece of paper proving he existed.

The Denver Rescue Mission tells the story of a man named Billy, who had lived in a park for four months before finding his way to their Lawrence Street Community Center. Staff helped him access his birth certificate, apply for an ID, and land a custodial job at a motel. He hasn't panhandled since. "I'm used to working," he said. "I've been working since I was 15, and I'm 51 now. Now that I'm going to have this job, it's great to be able to start all over again."

2

Give Something Useful Instead of, or Alongside, Cash

If you're skeptical about how cash will be used, or just want your generosity to go further, consider carrying a small kit. None of this is expensive, and all of it addresses documented needs.

The Practical Giving Kit

•Snack bars, trail mix, or peanut butter crackers — calorie-dense, non-perishable, no utensils required
•Socks — the most requested item at nearly every shelter, and among the least donated
•A bus pass or transit card — enables getting to a shelter, job interview, or service provider
•A printed resource card — local shelter address, food bank hours, and the 211 helpline number

The 211 number is worth knowing yourself. Dialing 211 connects callers to local health and human services — food, housing, employment, crisis intervention — for free, in most states. It's the most underused resource in social services.

3

Don't Underestimate the Value of Basic Dignity

Research on panhandling consistently surfaces one unexpected finding: panhandlers describe being ignored as one of the hardest parts of their day. Not the cold, not the uncertainty, not the money — the invisibility. One Washington D.C. panhandler quoted in a sociological study called the people who walked past without acknowledging her "zombies."

You don't have to give money. But making eye contact, saying "not today" or "I can't help with cash but here's a resource," treating the person as a human being asking a question — this costs nothing and matters more than people realize.

4

Support Organizations That Address the Cascade

If you want your money to do more than a dollar can, give to organizations that specialize in breaking the prerequisite chain. Habitat for Humanity and local housing nonprofits address the foundational shelter problem. United Way funds a web of local service organizations, including many that work on ID recovery and case management.

Street newspapers — like StreetWise in Chicago, Real Change in Seattle, and dozens of others across the country — let homeless vendors buy papers wholesale and sell them at a markup, creating legitimate, dignified income with organizational support. Your $20 to a well-run local shelter does orders of magnitude more work than $20 handed directly to someone whose needs are systemic, not transactional.

5

If They're a Veteran, There's a Specific Path

About one in four panhandlers in the U.S. has served in the military. For veterans, there's a clear, well-funded system that most aren't accessing: the VA's network of Homeless Coordinators can provide a VA office address for banking, connect veterans to the HUD-VASH housing voucher program, and link them to healthcare and disability benefits they may be owed but haven't claimed.

VA National Call Center for Homeless Veterans

1-877-4AID-VET  (1-877-424-3838) Free  ·  24 hours a day  ·  7 days a week

The Savings Question: For Later, Not Now

Here's something worth holding onto: the logic of saving a small fraction of every dollar you earn is genuinely powerful. The habit of setting aside even a modest percentage — consistently, before it can be spent — is the foundation of financial stability. That logic doesn't stop being true because someone is poor.

But it's premature advice for someone who doesn't yet have a bank account, a safe place to sleep, or a consistent income stream. Telling someone to save before they have those basics is like telling someone to exercise before treating a broken leg.

The right sequence is:

Documents → Address → Banking access → Steady income → Savings habits
The compounding math doesn't care what you started with. It only cares how early you started and whether you kept going.

Once someone has a steady job — even minimum wage — setting aside even 10% automatically, month after month, starts doing real work. A person earning $15 an hour who saves that fraction consistently is doing something that most Americans never manage. The math is patient and indifferent. It rewards the habit, not the income level.

That's a conversation for later. The conversation for right now is: know the local mission's address, carry a few resource cards, and treat the person on the corner like they exist.

The One Thing

Find out the name and address of one homeless service organization in your city and write it down. That's it.

The next time you stop at a light, hand over a dollar, or feel that familiar mix of sympathy and helplessness, you'll have something useful to add. That information — offered with a moment of genuine human contact — is worth more than the dollar. It might be worth quite a lot more.

Sources

Peer-reviewed research from the Canadian Medical Association Journal (Bose & Hwang, 2002); SAGE Journals systematic review of panhandling income (Reinhard, 2023); ASU Center for Problem-Oriented Policing; Northeast Ohio Coalition for the Homeless; Denver Rescue Mission; U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs; NIH/PubMed Central; Snopes fact-check on panhandling income.
